title: Linear vs. Cosine Noise Schedules
concept: A linear schedule adds noise at a constant rate; a cosine schedule adds noise more gently at the start and end, often producing better results.

exercises.prompt: When a diffusion model 'adds noise' to an image over many steps, what do you think happens by the very last step?

options.text: The image becomes pure random static
The image stays exactly the same

exercises.explain: Yes! By the final step, the original picture is completely buried under random noise, like TV static.

cards.title: Not All Noise Floods Arrive the Same Way
cards.body: Imagine slowly filling a bathtub with water. You could pour it in at a steady rate the whole time... or pour slowly at first, faster in the middle, then slowly again at the end. Diffusion models face the same choice when adding noise — and the choice they make is called a 'noise schedule'.

cards.title: What Is a Noise Schedule?
cards.body: A noise schedule is the plan for HOW MUCH noise gets added at each step while training a diffusion model. A LINEAR schedule adds noise at a constant, steady rate the whole time. A COSINE schedule adds noise gently at the start and end, but a bit faster through the middle — following a smooth curve instead of a straight line.

cards.title: Steeping Tea
cards.body: Linear schedule is like dunking a tea bag and stirring at the exact same speed the whole time. Cosine schedule is like dipping it gently at first, swirling faster in the middle, then slowing down again near the end — so the tea doesn't get too strong too quickly. That gentle start and end gives the model more useful steps to learn from.

cards.title: Training a Face Generator
cards.body: With a LINEAR schedule, a face might turn into total static surprisingly fast, wasting many steps on 'already-ruined' images. With a COSINE schedule, the face stays recognizable a bit longer near the start, giving the model more helpful steps to learn small details — often leading to sharper, more realistic generated faces.

cards.title: Myth: More Noise Steps Always = Better
cards.body: Myth: Adding noise faster or in bigger jumps always trains a model better. Truth: If noise is added too fast (like a bad linear schedule), the image becomes pure static too early, wasting training steps. Cosine schedules spread useful, learnable noise levels more evenly, which is why they often beat plain linear schedules.

cards.title: Straight Line vs. Smooth Curve
cards.body: Picture a graph of 'how much noise' (up) versus 'time step' (across). A linear schedule is a straight diagonal line. A cosine schedule is a smooth curve that starts flat, rises through the middle, and flattens again near the end.

left.text: Straight line — noise increases at the same constant rate every step

right.text: Smooth curve — gentle noise increase at start & end, faster in the middle

exercises.prompt: What is the main difference between a linear and a cosine noise schedule?

options.text: Linear adds noise at a constant rate; cosine adds it more gently at the start and end
Linear never adds noise; cosine always adds noise
Cosine only works on audio, not images
They are two names for the exact same schedule

exercises.explain: Linear = steady constant rate. Cosine = gentle at the edges, a bit faster in the middle, following a smooth curve.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Cosine noise schedules often produce better results than linear schedules in diffusion models.
exercises.explain: True! Because cosine schedules avoid destroying the image too quickly, models often learn more useful information across training steps.

exercises.sentence: A ____ schedule adds noise at a constant, steady rate across every step.
exercises.explain: A linear schedule increases noise steadily and evenly, like a straight diagonal line on a graph.

exercises.prompt: Which schedule tends to turn an image into pure static TOO QUICKLY, wasting learnable training steps?

options.text: Neither — both are identical in speed

exercises.explain: Linear schedules can ramp up noise so fast that images become unrecognizable static early, leaving fewer useful steps to learn fine details.

exercises.prompt: A student says: 'It doesn't matter which noise schedule you pick — linear and cosine always give identical training results.' What's wrong with this claim?

options.text: Nothing — the claim is correct, schedules never affect results
The claim is wrong: cosine schedules add noise more gently at the start/end, often preserving more useful detail and leading to better results than linear
The claim is wrong because cosine schedules don't use time steps at all
The claim is wrong because linear schedules remove noise instead of adding it

exercises.explain: Correct! The shape of the noise schedule matters. Cosine's gentler start/end curve often helps models learn better than a straight-line linear schedule.
